data.title: Qudian: Balance Sheet Strength Gives The Income Statement Time To Improve
data.description:
Qudian (NYSE:QD), a provider of credit, logistics and other solutions in China, has dropped in recent weeks as the market came to grips with the realization that the U.S. and China appear to be heading towards a trade war due to a dispute surrounding tariffs. This is expected to impact the global economy, and QD by extension, due to its exposure to it. It didn’t help that QD reported weakness in its most recent quarterly report, with the company suffering a quarterly loss, which further strengthens the perception the stock is heading towards rough waters. Nonetheless, there is a case to be made to be long QD. Why will be covered next.

QD has fallen, but support may be close

The bulls got something to cheer about last November when the stock finally managed to break past the $2.40-2.60 price region, something QD tried and failed to do numerous times starting in July 2023. Note how the stock reversed course several times once it got to the $2.40-2.60 region in the chart below, only to break through the aforementioned region in November. QD went on to hit a 52-weeks high of $3.34 as recently as February 14, but the stock has since fallen off to close at $2.19 per ADS on April 8.

The decline coincided with talk of the U.S. imposing tariffs on other countries, including China, where QD is from. This talk took down not just QD, but the stock market as a whole. The decline in the stock accelerated in early April after the Trump Administration officially announced tariffs as part of “Liberation day.”

However, the stock may have encountered support. Note how in the above chart, the stock rallied from a low of $1.52 to a high of $3.34. If we then assume the stock has been retracing this move up, then the 61.8% Fibonacci retracement of $1.52 to $3.34 is $2.22, $0.03 away from $2.19 where the stock currently resides. In other words, potential support is close by, which could be enough to power a bounce in the stock.

QD is also in oversold territory with, for instance, an RSI reading in the twenties after the stock has fallen by almost 35% compared to the February high of $3.34. If we combine all this, being oversold and close to a potential support level, then QD might actually be at a good entry point for those who are considering long QD.

Why QD could be undervalued

There is another reason why QD deserves at least a look besides the charts. A stock price of $2.19 per ADS implies a market cap of $414.8M, since the total number of ADRs is about 189.4M. In comparison, QD has cash and cash equivalents of $584.1M, according to the most recent report or Q4 FY2024 report, which converts to $3.08 per ADS. QD has more cash and cash equivalents than what the stock goes for.

True, QD has about $112.8M of total debt, which includes $98.6M of short-term borrowings on the balance sheet, but this is countered by other items on the balance sheet, including restricted cash totaling $107M, time and structured deposits totaling $275.2M and short-term investments totaling $153.2M.

In all, QD has a book value of $1,546.92M with total assets of $1,702.4M and total liabilities of $155.48M, which converts to a book value of $8.17 per ADS. AD is therefore available at less than book value, with a price-to-book of just 0.27x and a share price of $2.19 per ADS. QD can thus be argued to be undervalued based on the most recent balance sheet.

The income statement at QD needs work

The balance sheet is in good shape, but the same cannot necessarily be said of the income statement. For instance, the legacy business has shrunk over the years, although this is due to the winding down of the micro-lending business. As a result, revenue shrank for four consecutive years to fall from a peak of $1.27B to just $18M in FY2023, the lowest since QD went public in 2017. The stock has been similarly affected. QD went for $24 per ADS at the time, which gives one a sense of how much the stock has fallen over the years.

Net income also fell during this time. Net income peaked at a post-IPO high of $1.57 per ADS in FY2019, only to shrink to just $0.03 per ADS in FY2023, which was again the end result of QD abandoning the lending business. This changed in the recently concluded FY2024 when both the top and the bottom line showed improvement with QD reporting a net profit of $0.07 per ADS on revenue of $29.65M, up from $0.03 on revenue of $18M in the preceding FY2023.

TTM net income of $0.07 a share translates to a P/E ratio of 31.3x, with the stock at $2.19 per ADS. This seems high, but keep in mind that this is for a stock that more than doubled earnings YoY, even if it was versus a low base. If QD can sustain fast growth, then a relatively high multiple can be justified.

QD is trying to get back to growth by moving into another market

This resurgence of growth can be attributed to QD's search for new growth drivers, particularly last-mile delivery to facilitate e-commerce in places like Australia and New Zealand. However, while the full-year numbers look okay, the latest report shows some troubling signs. QD actually incurred a loss of $0.05 in the last quarter of FY2024 due to intense competition. The Q4 loss of $0.05 matches that of the loss of $0.05 in Q1, which partially offset the profits of $0.07 and $0.10 a share in Q2 and Q3, respectively.

Note also that if not for interest income from its substantial cash reserves, losses would have been bigger. AD suffered a $42.2M loss from operations in FY2024, but $52.07M income from interests and investments made up for it and was the main reason why QD reported a net income of $12.57M in FY2024.

The income statement has been uneven in recent quarters, although QD does have a better record in the longer term. The balance sheet shows a positive balance of $1,187.8M in terms of retained earnings, which shows QD has been able to generate more profits than losses over its lifetime as a listed stock. QD deserves the benefit of the doubt for this reason.

The last-mile delivery business is very much a work in progress that has only been rolled out in a few markets, and it is probably too early to pass judgment. Still, the last-mile delivery business is seen as the future of QD, but results have been uneven, and it is not impossible the venture will not succeed. If this happens, QD will have to look for some other business venture to power growth in the coming years.

QD faces risks from other corners

The last-mile delivery business needs to do better, and QD is working on it, but the stock could get hit from another direction. QD is a Chinese stock listed on a U.S. exchange and the issue here is that with China and the U.S. in a major trade dispute, the possibility arises the U.S. could pass punitive measures, which could include the delisting of Chinese stocks, including QD.

The Trump Administration recently declined to rule out the possibility of delisting when asked, which means investors have to account for the risk of this happening when evaluating whether they ought to get in on QD. This could weigh on the ability of the stock to move forward and could even pressure it down. Note that on previous occasions, like when there was a dispute about accounting rules with Chinese companies years ago, the U.S. government ultimately decided not to proceed with delisting of Chinese stocks.

The stock is also an ADR/ADS, which gives limited rights in the event there is a dispute pertaining to shareholders. It is possible that with the current environment where Chinese stocks are perceived to be at elevated risk due to the U.S.-China trade dispute, combined with the limitations of an instrument like ADR/ADS, some, if not all, investors, may decide to stay away, at least for the time being.

Investor takeaways

QD is a company that has seen its legacy business shrink in recent years, and it is therefore trying to refashion itself by moving into new businesses like e-commerce with last-mile delivery. Yet, doing so requires time and substantial investments, which can be seen in the income statements from QD. QD has incurred operating losses, including in the Q4 FY2024 report, but QD has been able to negate these losses thanks to its cash reserves, which is why QD has stayed out of the red in terms of the bottom line on an annual basis.

The stock’s performance has been weighed down by a declining top and bottom line in recent years. The stock fell again after it became clear the U.S. was set to impose tariffs on other countries, including China. QD is a Chinese stock listed in the U.S. at the end of the day and the U.S.-China trade dispute could have negative consequences for QD.

Trade could shrink, which could affect QD’s last-mile delivery business, which is already under pressure. The stock could also be affected by rising tensions between the U.S. and China if investors become wary of investing in Chinese stocks when there is the risk of them being subjected to U.S. sanctions, like being delisted. All this is likely to affect the stock, especially in the short term, since the trade dispute has yet to be resolved.

However, an argument can be made that long QD is warranted despite the risks hanging over QD. The stock has dropped in recent weeks, but this also means the stock is arguably undervalued at current levels. The income statement needs improvement, but the balance sheet is strong enough that QD has time to be patient with the last-mile delivery business. QD has enough income-generating assets that can offset the losses incurred as the company tries to grow the last-mile delivery to the point it can stand on its own.

And even if the foray into e-commerce does not work out or if QD decides to wind down the business to try something else, QD has enough assets to start fresh. In fact, there are enough assets on the books to warrant a higher valuation than the current $2.19 per ADS. QD has enough cash for the stock to trade above $3 per ADS, or well above where it is available right now, and close to its most recent high of $3.38 per ADS.

QD needs some of the cash to invest in and expand new growth drivers, which is why the stock is unlikely to trade near its current book value of $8.19 per ADS. Yet for QD to be valued at just about a quarter of book value is arguably too low, considering that QD continues to be profitable overall and QD has a solid track record in terms of generating profits. While QD needs to work on the income statement, it also deserves to be given the benefit of the doubt that it can get it done, whether it is the last-mile delivery business or whatever QD decides to do in the future.

QD might actually be at a good entry point right now in terms of the charts. The stock is oversold after falling 35% in recent weeks, and it is close to an important Fibonacci retracement level, which could offer support since buyers are more likely to step in at a price level that potentially offers support. If someone thought about long QD, but was unsure when to step in, then the charts suggest QD is at point where those who are new to the stock or those considering adding to existing holdings can make a move.

I am bullish on QD all things considered. There are certainly risks out there for QD and the evolving trade dispute between China and the US needs to be watched, but with the strength of the balance sheet, QD has a good shot at reaching the $3 per ADS target or about a third above where it last closed. QD also has the potential to go much higher if it can combine all the assets it has on the balance sheet with improvement in the income statement, particularly as it relates to the last-mile delivery business.

In the end, the balance sheet makes up for the income statement. Longs may need to be patient because the global trade situation could subject QD to uncertainty for some time, which could hold back the stock. The income statement needs work, but with the balance sheet as is, those long QD can afford to be patient.

data.title: Avino Silver & Gold: After Quick Double, Still Provides Powerful Metals Leverage
data.description:
One of my bigger winners over the past several years has been Avino Silver & Gold Mines Ltd. (NYSE:ASM). My last bullish article mention was in November here. Since that effort, its share price has roughly doubled, as everyone is coming to the conclusion President Trump's policies are not a net positive for the country or the world. People are questioning whether the U.S. dollar should be the global reserve currency, and if US$36 trillion in sovereign Treasury debt is remotely serviceable.

Not only did ASM's Q4 earnings and sales beat by a wide margin (both approximately besting Wall Street estimates by 50%), but the company has received permit approval to expand operations and output at the recently acquired (and next-door property) La Preciosa mine. From the March 12th earnings release,

... in January, we announced the receipt of necessary approvals to move ahead with the underground development at La Preciosa, one of the largest undeveloped silver deposits in Mexico and the world. Equipment mobilization and surface works have been ongoing with underground development getting underway. La Preciosa is an integral piece to our five-year plan and will deliver economic growth and benefit to the local communities in Durango ...

The really good news for shareholders is the fact Avino should be able to self-fund a large increase in production over the next few years. While most miners have to borrow money or issues shares (diluting existing owners) to bring on new production, ASM has $27 million in cash, no debt, and should generate between $30-40 million in cash flow during 2025 (per management production estimates and the current silver/gold/copper price level).

Avino is a Canadian company owning a silver mining complex in Mexico. So, its operations are entirely out of the reach of schizophrenic Trump tariff trade policies in the U.S. The nominal value of 371 million silver-equivalent ounces in the ground (for measured & indicated resources) is almost $85 per share vs. a quote of $2 (42:1 leverage). For comparison, major miners are generally in the 10:1 to 15:1 leverage range today.

With an estimated $49 million required to sharply increase production levels, ASM is in tremendously better financial shape to proceed with La Preciosa's development than 18-24 months ago.

The goals for production growth are rather eye popping. Management believes it can increase annual silver-equivalent ounces pulled out of the ground by 200%+ over the next four years. The highlight for investors is most of the new ounces will be actual silver brought to market (as opposed to the byproducts of gold and copper), possibly at even higher metals pricing in the near future.

Growth Underestimated

I will say Wall Street analysts appear to be completely underestimating the positives taking place at Avino, just like they did during 2024. Current financial projections for 2025-26 are nowhere close to management production guidance crossed with still rising precious metals pricing in April.

My view is $90+ million in sales (2.6 million silver-equivalent oz. multiplied by an average $35 silver price for 2025) and $0.15 in GAAP EPS will be reality for 2025. Next year, I am projecting $125+ million in sales and $0.30 EPS, assuming the projected 40% increase in production over the current rate is accomplished with similar unit mining costs.

With silver going for US$32 an ounce and gold at $3230 oz, the gold/silver ratio has risen above 100x! Only a few weeks during the 2020 pandemic panic have ever witnessed a more stretched ratio over the last 110 years.

In addition, a record short position now exists in silver-based bullion ETFs. Traders are not expecting a significant rush of safety-minded investors to show up, but they may if the U.S. dollar starts to tank. Seeking Alpha analyst Chirs Puplava wrote an excellent opinion piece last week here on the bullish arguments for a major silver push higher in 2025.

Mathematically, it is becoming "almost" certain silver will start to outperform gold over the coming year or two. And, I have explained in other articles that US$100 or even $200 silver will eventually become reality sooner or later, as Treasury debt will have to be monetized with excessive levels of money printing in America.

So, if a monster silver rally does play out this year or next, ASM could be one of the top risk-adjusted leveraged mining bets to put money in your pocket.

The debt-free balance sheet, super-strong projected growth in production, and unusual silver leverage in the ground could create another double this year for its stock. Perhaps even another double of that price to $8 or $10 a share in 2026, assuming silver prices are set to explode.

Using management guidance for 8 million silver-equivalent ounces of annual production by 2029 and $100 for a future silver price (with gold at $5000 oz. and copper at $8 per pound), $800 million in company revenue with $350 million in after-tax profits per annum is not entirely science fiction.

This compares to an equity market capitalization of $280 million and enterprise value closer to $250 million. If all goes according to management plan, and silver/gold/copper continue to rise, calling for a $10-20 ASM share quote years from now is well within reach.

Final Thoughts

What are the risks owning Avino Silver & Gold? The first is obvious. If silver/gold/copper prices decline, it will be hard for ASM to increase in price much above $2.

Another existential risk comes from its Mexico mine location. Conventional wisdom is Mexico has been turning against mining concerns in terms of regulation and tax policy. However, with President Trump in office, I actually prefer owning established precious metals assets outside the United States.

A simple calculation in how the markets (and I) value gold/silver miners with strong reserves and economic operations (earning a profit), utilizing little debt, is to compare the current P/E vs. the number of years of reserves at present operating rates. More often than not (over 38 years of trading the sector), these figures are close to balance.

For example, Agnico Eagle (AEM) has in the neighborhood of 20-25 years of economic reserves and resources at $3200 gold (27 years if including ALL proven & probable reserves plus measured & indicated resources), alongside a P/E in the low 20s. The two ideas about match, meaning I consider AEM to be selling closer to full value, where the share quote will fluctuate with gold prices.

Conversely, I am big holder of Newmont (NEM), with 30-35 years of economic reserves at 2025's projected production level. However, I am now estimating NEM to have a P/E of 14x ($55 quote divided by $4 EPS) using prevailing gold/silver/copper quotes. This is a huge disconnect arguing the stock remains massively undervalued.

For ASM, the $2 price divided by my $0.15 EPS forecast works out to 13x for a P/E ratio. At 2.6 million equivalent silver ounces for production, 371 million in measured & indicated resources works out to 142 years of inground potential. My conservative view is the company has 40+ years of economic reserves at the 2025 production rate and close to 14 years at the 2029 projected rate of 8 million silver-equivalent ounces (which would be 3x the level of 2.6 million ounces).

Using this valuation method, ASM should be trading between a range of $4 and $7 today (depending on the variable inputs of future metals pricing and actual mining costs), not $2 per share.

That's why I put a Strong Buy rating on Avino Silver & Gold around $1 per share in November, and am reiterating the company's ultra-bullish outlook again at $2 in April 2025 for long-term precious metal thinkers.

Avino has trounced both the S&P 500 and gold/silver mining indexes over the past 18 months for investor returns. I believe the same outperformance trend will continue during 2025-26.

Thanks for reading. Please consider this article a first step in your due diligence process. Consulting with a registered and experienced investment advisor is recommended before making any trade.

data.title: MTY Food Group: At 4.3x Cash Flow, MTY Still Looks Undervalued
data.description:
Note: Unless otherwise indicated, all monetary values are presented in Canadian dollars.

A Quiet Hero in Tough Times

They say a dog is man’s best friend—and hey, I won’t argue with that. But when it comes to restaurants? Sure, some might say it’s all about value. But sometimes, it’s the asset-light model that really comes to the rescue.

Just ask MTY Food Group (OTCPK:MTYFF) (TSX:MTY:CA).

The year kicked off with a bang, hinting at a real comeback after a Q4 FY 2024 unlike anything the industry had seen in at least two years. But then—quite literally—the headwinds rolled in. Wildfires in California and freezing temperatures in the Southeast ended up severely impacting foot traffic in two key regions for most restaurant chains.

February came and things got more complicated. The blizzards and wildfires had eased up, and regions like California, Florida, the Midwest, and the Southwest started to stand out.

But even so, the overall picture was far from great.

'Trump Tariffs' have not only made restaurateurs more worried—but consumers too. Consumer confidence might not be the best way to call a recession, but it’s definitely a good read on how the Discretionary sector is feeling.

It’s pretty simple: if I think prices are going up in six months, I’m going to hold on to my cash today. Eating at home or cheaper options like supermarkets, c-stores and those things eat up most of the discretionary spending that previously flowed to restaurants.

And that’s bad for traffic.

But guess who’s suffering the most? Bingo, fast food. It’s no wonder that same-store sales at QSRs are falling faster than the rest of the industry. It hits harder when you zoom in on lower-income households.

But all is not lost. BBI said March data was stronger than February, likely returning to January levels.

In choppy waters like these, one quiet hero stands tall: the asset-light model.

Years of following companies like Dine Brands (DIN) and you probably know the formula: Lower than usual sales (reflecting franchisee royalties and other fees), stronger margins (you're not as exposed to rising operating costs), stable operating cash flow, reduced Capex, and exceptionally strong free cash flow.

You'll also usually see dividends and buybacks.

While growth investors tend to avoid these guys, they often offer a safe haven in tough times.

Sure, you might see some net closures as franchisees shut down underperforming locations—but overall, your returns take a much smaller hit than they would at chains loaded with corporate-owned stores.

Take MTY, for example—70 openings were more than offset by 102 closures, for a net decline of 32 locations. And yet, their operating cash flow grew by nearly double digits. Once again: thank you, asset-light model.

Let's see this in practice

In Q1 FY 2025, MTY reported revenues of $284.8 million—up 2.2% year-over-year.

At first glance, this increase might be misleading, but remember that this was driven by MTY’s resumption of some underperforming stores in the second half of FY 2024 in both Canada and the United States.

Looking at franchises, they reported a 2% year-over-year decline in Canada, offset by a 2% year-over-year increase in the United States. On the bottom line, they reported a 3.1% year-over-year decline in organic sales.

As for operating costs, they increased in both Canada and the United States—2% and 3% year-over-year, respectively. On the other hand, management did a good job of reducing $2.1 million in SG&A expenses. This helped maintain the franchise segment’s EBITDA margin at 47%, growing 1% year-over-year. Corporate store margins also remained flat year over year, in a range of 10% to 12%.

When you look at the P&L, it’s easy to think the results are pretty bad—especially with EPS dropping to $0.07, down over 90% from last year’s diluted EPS of $0.71. But it turns out this drop was mainly due to accounting for currency fluctuations on intercompany loans, which don’t actually involve any cash movement. Normalizing that diluted EPS to Q2 FY 2025, we would have $0.87. So it grew 22.5% year over year!

And here’s the magic. Even with the increase in operating costs, MTY reported OCF of $58.8 million, growing 8.7% year over year. With $3.2 million in Capex, they reported FCF of $55.6 million. After reducing its debt by $8.7 million and keeping it below the targeted 2.5x Debt/EBITDA ratio, MTY returned $21.4 million in dividends and buybacks.

Nothing’s compromised.

With that kind of strong free cash flow, they can keep serving up tasty returns to shareholders.

-14% YTD: Is This Canadian Giant Still Cheap?

As you probably know by now, my target’s in the high $30s to low $40s range. With the outlook still cloudy for many restaurant chains, we might not hit that target anytime soon.

But hey—we’re in this for the long run. Like I’ve said before, asset-light restaurant chains can be a safe haven when things get rough. And who doesn’t love a steady, reliable dividend? I know I do.

In my opinion, the chances of dividend cuts or reductions are quite low here.

I know, I know, management has not given any guidance for all of FY 2025. Still, they’ve made it clear they plan to keep Capex at the lower levels we saw in FY 2024, which was unusually high due to repayments tied to a few ill-fated acquisitions.

With no deals currently on the table, they don’t have many options besides returning capital to shareholders. The buyback program is already bumping up against TSX limits, and if an acquisition does show up, they’ll likely just trim the return accordingly.

Enough talk, let’s model some scenarios. Remember, I will now be using US$ to model this.

Since we have no guidance, let's settle for some assumptions:

  1. Revenues of US$835.36 million for FY 2025 and US$844.88 million for FY 2026;
  2. EBITDA margin at 20% and EBIT margin at 15% based on post-pandemic figures;
  3. OCF margin from 16% to 19%.

The first thing I want to point out here is how low MTY’s P/CF is currently trading.

To give you an idea, with an OCF of US$146.3 million and about 23.4 million shares outstanding in FY 2024, we would have an OCF per share of US$6.25. That would give us a P/CF of 4.52x! That is almost 50% below the industry median of 8.75x and the same distance from the 5-year average.

If MTY were trading at the industry median, we would have a price of US$54.68. Quite a difference, right?

But let me take a moment to look at the future growth trend of this OCF. Considering the projected revenue for FY 2025 and an OCF margin of 17.5%, we would be looking at an OCF of US$146.18 million. Also, taking into account repurchases of approximately 1 million shares outstanding for FY 2025, we would have an OCF per share of US$6.52.

In other words, at today's price, MTY stock would be trading at a P/CF of 4.33x based on the projections for FY 2025.

Now, looking at FCF, MTY reported $128.7 million for FY 2024. With 23.4 million shares outstanding, this would give us FCF per share of $5.50. This would give us a P/FCF ratio of 5.14x.

If we look at other asset-light restaurant chains, we find the following results: Dine Brands (3.2x), A & W (AW:CA) (12.45x), Restaurant Brands International (QSR) (15.68x), Yum! Brands (YUM) (28.26x), Jack in the Box (JACK) (5.47x), Nathan's Famous (NATH) (15.36x).

Looking ahead and considering OCF at US$146.18 million and Capex in line with FY 2022 (US$6.4 million), we would have FCF per share of US$6.24. This would give us a P/FCF of 4.53x, still below the average of its asset-light peers.

So yes. MTY is still cheap based on its OCF and FCF projections — which are the best ways to analyze stocks of this type. But why? Well, EPS can be volatile and distorted by non-cash items, and even EBITDA or EBIT can get a little messy for the same reasons.

That said, I reiterate my 'Buy' rating here.

Sure, the macro backdrop isn’t great, and there’s still room for risk assets to dip. But that just means more chances to load up on reliable, steady dividend payers — the kind that help cushion your portfolio when growth stocks start tumbling.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: J&J Snack Foods: Despite Some Promising Signs, I'm Staying Away For Now
data.description:
Times have not been pleasant for J&J Snack Foods (NASDAQ:JJSF). The company, which sells major brand names such as Superpretzel, Brauhaus, Dippin' Dots, ICEE, and more, has taken a heck of a beating in recent months. Back in late December, I acknowledged that the company had been exhibiting improvements in some key areas. But the most recent financial data, which covered the final quarter of the 2024 fiscal year, showed some undesired weakness. This, combined with how the stock was priced, caused me to argue that the company made for a better ‘hold’ than it did a ‘buy.’ However, even that assessment seems to have been overly optimistic. Since that time, shares of the company have plummeted by 14.9%. That's far worse than the 9.4% decline experienced by the S&P 500 over the same window of time.

Since then, we have seen additional data come out. The revenue picture has picked up some. Management also seems optimistic about the impact that the recovery of the box office might have on the firm. Unfortunately, bottom-line results have worsened during this time. This, combined with how the stock continues to look from a valuation perspective, has me confident that maintaining a more neutral position is appropriate. Shares have certainly gotten cheaper since my previous article. But they aren't cheap enough to become optimistic just yet.

Not tasty enough

When I last wrote about J&J Snack Foods late last year, investors had access to data covering through the final quarter of 2024. The data that we have gotten since then covers the first quarter of 2025. During that time, revenue for the company came in at $362.6 million. That's a 4.1% increase over the $348.3 million generated one year earlier. This rise in revenue was driven by strength across all three of the company's operating segments.

Most important was the Food Service segment, which as its name suggests, caters to food service companies such as snack bars and food stands that are located in chain, department, and discount stores, malls and shopping centers, fast food and casual dining restaurants, stadiums, theme parks, convenience stores, movie theaters, and more. Year over year, revenue for this segment expanded by 4.5% from $228.6 million to $238.9 million.

Even within this segment, there were weak spots and spots of strength. Most impressive was the 9.8% surge in frozen novelties products that the company experienced. Revenue there grew from $21.1 million to $23.1 million, thanks to strong growth from the company’s Dippin' Dots brand. Handhelds revenue grew 7.5% from $22 million to $23.7 million. This was because of higher prices aimed at combating raw material ingredient cost increases that the company had to contend with. However, sales volumes also improved during this time.

Bakery sales popped 6.6% from $102 million to $108.7 million. Management attributed this to pricing true-ups aimed at adjusting for higher raw material ingredients costs, as well as volume growth for its cookie products. Meanwhile, soft pretzel sales grew by 4.8% from $50.1 million to $52.5 million. This is because of higher volumes under this category. The only major weak spot involved churros. Revenue for that category dropped 9.2% from $28.1 million to $25.5 million. Management blamed this on the fact that, in the previous year, sales had been temporarily pumped up for a limited time by quick serve restaurants that were promoting them.

Other segments did fine as well. The company’s Retail Supermarket segment saw revenue inch up by 2.2% from $43.8 million to $44.7 million. While Dippin' Dots caused frozen novelties revenue to skyrocket by 25.3%, there was a 7.4% drop in soft pretzel sales. These fell from $18.4 million to $17.1 million. The good news here is that this pain should be transitory. Management claimed that the decline was because of a temporary issue with one of its major retail customers. They had some sort of ordering system problem that resulted in reduced volumes. According to the data provided, this problem was resolved in December of last year. In addition to this, the Frozen Beverages segment saw revenue climbed by 4% from $75.9 million to $79 million. Machines revenue jumped 13% from $8.9 million to $10 million thanks to strong demand from movie theaters and convenience customers. Beverages revenue grew by 6.4% from $42 million to $44.7 million. This was because of a 10% rise in gallon sales.

I wish I could say that the bottom line had improved as well. But that would be a lie. Net income for the company actually contracted from $7.3 million to $5.1 million. Unfortunately, the firm’s Food Service segment saw its operating income plummet 72.2% from $6 million to $1.7 million. This was because of rising raw material costs and other inflationary pressures that more than offset higher prices and rising volumes. It didn't help that there was an unfavorable change in product mix because the company lost some seasonal business within its bakery category. Other profitability metrics took a hit as well. Operating cash flow dropped from $49 million to $35.2 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a drop from $25.5 million to $23.9 million. Even EBITDA took a hit, dropping from $30.2 million to $25.3 million.

We don't really know what to expect when it comes to the rest of the fiscal year. Management hasn't provided much in the way of guidance. We do know that management is optimistic that the company could see additional revenue growth because of the box office in North America. The current expectation that they have is that box office sales should climb by 10% year over year for 2025. I actually think the picture might end up being a bit better than that. In early March, I wrote an article about AMC Entertainment Holdings (AMC). In that article, I looked at box office revenue for the first seven weeks of 2025. Year over year, revenues were up 21.7%. Of course, this is a very short window of time and results can be skewed by even a single film. Having said that, in that article, I also mentioned that the industry is expecting to see 15 more films come out this year than what was achieved last year. So in all likelihood, some year-over-year increase is in the cards. However, if we look at more recent data that covers the first 14 full weeks of the year, we get a decline now of 6.6%, so the strong start to 2025 may be giving way to weakness.

Speaking of movie theaters, I think that some optimism is deserved when it comes to the Dippin' Dots brand, despite the recent change in the trend of box office results. During the quarter, the company saw the addition of 186 theater locations where this product is offered. In the most recent quarterly earnings conference call, management even expressed that there was ‘strong early acceptance’ from a national player and that additional locations will be expanded to in the second quarter of this year.

In the absence of any real guidance for the year as a whole, the best thing I can do is to value the company based on financial results for the last two fiscal years. In the chart above, you can see how the stock is currently priced on an absolute basis. I also, in the table below, stacked it up against five similar firms. As you can see in that table, three of the four companies are cheaper than J&J Snack Foods is on a price to adjusted operating cash flow basis and on an EV to EBITDA basis. This number rises to four of the five when using the price to earnings approach.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
J&J Snack Foods 29.1 14.9 12.7
Premium Brands Holdings Corp. (OTCPK:PRBZF) 27.3 13.1 10.1
Nomad Foods (NOMD) 12.6 6.6 8.4
Utz Brands (UTZ) 71.3 10.1 13.7
WK Kellogg Co (KLG) 23.4 16.6 10.6
The Simply Good Foods Company (SMPL) 25.7 18.4 14.8

Takeaway

In my opinion, J&J Snack Foods is not some home run prospect. It's also not a horrible business. Shares have certainly gotten cheaper since I last reaffirmed the company as a ‘hold’ candidate. But they haven't gotten cheap enough as yet to justify a bullish assessment. Despite some signs of growth that are occurring, particularly when it comes to its Dippin' Dots brand, I believe that maintaining a ‘hold’ rating on the stock is the most sensible choice I can make at this time.

data.title: Dollar General: The Mother Of All Catalysts
data.description:
There’s no hiding behind the bush. Dollar General (NYSE:DG) has had a rough patch. Over the past 28 months it's been a textbook case of poor execution spiralling into poor sentiment. Missed numbers. Top-management turnover. Margin erosion. Inventory missteps. Deteriorated in-store conditions. All of the above contributed to the stock being down 62% from its peak and underperforming the S&P500 by 90%. and its respective industry benchmark by 70%. Many see this business as a structural short. I believe that's misread. My forecast points to 32% return and 15% IRR by 2027 from current levels.

I believe Dollar General is still one of the most advantaged retailers in the US and is at the cusp of a powerful point of inflection driven by a powerful cloud looming over the macro environment; tariffs. While the entire consumer industry is bracing for margin compression and sourcing chaos, Dollar General has some key advantages deeply engrained in its business model that make it stand out as a relative winner. Tariffs threaten to punish the players that leaned too heavily on discretionary imports and apparently fragile supply chains. Dollar General does not. The company sources most of its goods from the US and its sales mix is skewed towards non-discretionary essentials. In this unfolding wave of deglobalization, Dollar General’s model is not a design flaw, it’s an edge that finally has a catalyst to make it shine.

Big scale, small-town DNA

DG operates over 20,000 stores across 48 states in the US and Mexico, in mostly rural and underserved areas. Its vast footprint makes it the largest discount retailer in America. Over 80% of the company’s sales come from consumables such as paper products, packed food, personal care and pet supplies. The core customer base is made out of low-income households that are typically underserved by traditional retailers. The stores are low-cost, small box, ~7,500 sq. ft. on average, and strategically located in suburban rural communities where big-box competitors have a tough time scaling. Over the past 24 months, management’s execution faltered. Shop lifting, also known as shrinking worsened. Inventory was miss-managed and store managed turnover rose. Of course, the market took note. However, the market is overlooking that the store format still resonates with consumers, and that new management is taking impactful actions to triage the issues with distribution upgrades, selective store remodelling and SKU rationalization.

Durability of the model

This isn’t about chasing a rebound in a broken business model. My process is rooted in asymmetry, finding resilient businesses with misunderstood setups, and clear catalysts that realign fundamentals and sentiment. That’s exactly what we are facing here.

The model has long relied on small-box simplicity. Low cost and fast buildouts, lean inventory management, swift replenishment, and high-frequency traffic. The basket size has consistently been around $17 and Dollar General serves non-discretionary habitual needs, which provides natural insulation from economic downturns. To put that in context, Dollar General outgrew not just retailers broadly, but even its closest dollar-store peers during the 08-09 financial crisis. The table below demonstrates that point clearly.

The defensive core of the business is now being sharpened again. After a period of bloated inventory and operational missteps, management established the back to basics plan to stabilize labor, and put clearer planograms in place. Traffic trends are also reflecting that, with Dollar General store traffic inflecting higher.

Another source of competitive edge is the quiet evolution of the supply chain. The fleet has expanded to over 2000 tractors and management has increased control over sourcing, logistics and theft. All contributing positively to the gross margin. The expansion in private label, reduction in shrink and instillation of SG&A discipline will also contribute to margin upside even before revenue reaccelerates.

All this to say, this is not a hope-filled strategic turnaround story. The strategy is grounded in tangible changes, with visible and accountable execution underway and tangible results beginning to surface in the numbers. Meanwhile, the market is pricing DG as a broken story, but I believe the structure, scale, and defensibility of the model are very much intact.

The mother of all catalysts

Tariffs are no longer just a theoretical macro risk. On April 4th, POTUS announced tariffs that shook the markets. Their implications are real and starting to bite. While most retailers are bracing for impact, Dollar General is by-design ready to withstand the impact, and maybe one of the very few players positioned to thrive.

I say few because many consumer-facing retailers, especially those in toys, apparel and seasonal merch, have begun to voice their concerns. Retailers that rely on global sourcing are set to absorb a disproportionately larger impact compared to players with localized supply chains. For example, Five Below announced recently that 60% of the retailer’s total cost of goods sold are imported from China, with a disproportionately larger impact on $1-$5 price point items.

According to company filings, the direct cost-of-goods-sold exposure to imports is roughly 40% for Dollar Tree, mid-teens for family dollar and just 3-4% for Dollar General. For big box retailers such as Target (TGT), Costco (COST), Lowe's (LOW), Home Depot (HD) and Walmart (WMT), these figures are even higher, in the 30-50% range according to their filings. Given the nature of their offerings, I expect a substantial share of these imports to be from China, where tariffs are not only very high, but also likely very sticky.

The above structural advantage is strategic. While competitors are scrambling to replace and postpone deliveries or depress their margin to stay competitive, DG continues to focus on its strategy while minimizing the already small impact. Its core categories are not only defensive in nature, but they are also recurring and replenishment driven. As said earlier, over 80% of DG’s sales are in the essentials category, compared to below 60% for peers such as Dollar Tree and significantly less for Walmart etc. This inherent stability translates into less distraction and disruption and more predictable gross margins, with incrementally higher trust from the consumers about price visibility. This is particularly important at a time when we are already seeing an up-tick in trade-downs from consumers. All while DG store conditions are improving, inventory stability is restored, and the management team’s operational resets are amplifying this positive effect. While competitors are absorbing the tariff effect, DG is capturing the dislocated traffic.

In the meantime, the competitive environment is quietly cracking. It’s been reported that Family Dollar is closing down 1000 stores, Walgreens is shutting down 300 stores nationwide in 2025 and 99 Cents Only is also shrinking its footprint. Tariffs will further compress margins across retail, and that will create more whitespace for Dollar General to emerge stronger out of this situation. The edge is mix-driven and embedded in the model, ready to capitalize on this catalyst.

Some of these catalytical factors, such as management reset, operational clean up, and the trade-down dynamics - I’ve already touched on. But they are worth revisiting because they amplify the power of the tariff catalyst. Todd Vasos’ return has re-instilled a sharp focus on execution, with rationalization of stock keeping units, improving in stock rates and tidier store environments. Todd was the architect behind the golden stretch, from 2015-2022, where gross margins were stable and 200bps higher than the current, operating margins were stable and 400bps higher than the current, and the stock delivered a staggering 19% annualized return during his tenure.

The CEO’s initiatives are already having a positive impact on numbers. At the same time, broader distress in retail presents new opportunities for Dollar General to capture strategic retail space at the expense of Walgreens, Family Dollar and more. As these self-help levers begin to converge with the macro regime that has a disproportionately smaller impact on DG’s operations, the result is not just resilience, it’s the upside I exemplify below.

Valuation and risks

The assumptions underpinning my model are grounded in the competitive dynamics, catalysts and macro environment laid out throughout my analysis. From Dollar General’s relative tariff insulation and defensible category mix to execution reset underway, management’s proven credibility, and emerging competitive wins - each of these is reflected in my modelling. While my numbers are above consensus expectations, I am not painting a blue sky picture here. It’s a realistic acknowledgement of the structural advantages now reinforced by the mother of all catalysts in motion.

I also don’t view my valuation forecast as unreasonably aggressive. Anchoring my 2027 expectations on a 15x EV/EBIT exit multiple is reasonable especially considering that the historical average EV/EBIT is roughly 15.3x and the peak multiple over the past 5 years was 17x. Once the turnaround is in full force, the market will likely reflect that, particularly in a recessionary environment where capital tends to rotate into defensive business models and that are strategically well positioned to win.

Risk overview

Even through Dollar General has already made progress under Vasos’ leadership, the turnaround is still in its early stages and prone to execution missteps. Simplifying operations, improving inventory flow and resetting stores is difficult when there’s elevated shrink, and above average staff turnover. This industry has low pay and high quit rates, which make staff retention difficult and service levels prone to disruption. However, the back to basics plan is on the right path.

Walmart is a formidable player and is expanding into select rural markets. Dollar General has long been a beneficiary from white space between Walmart’s geographic positioning. Walmart has an aggressive pricing strategy and omnichannel convenience, which has the potential to dent DG’s traffic recovery. What gives me comfort with this risk is Walmart’s roughly 30% of COGS sourced from China, vs only 3-4% for DG. Based on company filings, the 20198 tariff cycle put roughly $55m of cost pressure per quarter on COGS that was eventually passed on to consumers. This indication should ease pricing pressure concerns for DG going forward.

Finally, DG is not fully immune to macro pressures. Rising costs of living can erode DG’s wallet share and slightly shrink basket sizes from the already low levels. However, in previous cycles, Dollar General has been a consistent beneficiary of middle-class households trade down volumes.

Conclusion

Dollar General is not an obvious trade. It’s a catalytical setup that reveals itself upon digging deeper into competitive positioning, sourcing, product mix and execution. While tariffs are drawing headlines are cause unprecedented volatility, their second-order effects are not yet fully understood. Dollar General’s structural insulation, combined with improving operations and strategic positioning, create a setup that’s not just defensive—it’s opportunistic. This is not about sentiment. It’s about fundamentals aligning with catalytical forces the market hasn’t fully priced in.

I see this as one of the most compelling and underappreciated catalyst-backed opportunities currently in the market that’s driven by increasingly tangible insight, and not pure narrative.

“Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best Stock With A Catalyst investment competition, which runs through April 17. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!”

data.title: Royal Gold: High Margins, Potential Growth Make It A Good Flight To Safety Stock
data.description:
"May you live in interesting times." It's an old phrase, though not actually an ancient Chinese curse as often attributed. I just thought that was a fun fact, what's less fun is that we are most assuredly living in interesting times.

Between tariff wars, trade disputes, and general global crisis, there is a growing concern we're headed into a global recession, or worse. That got me thinking about flight to safety-type stocks, and also about gold, the historic favorite asset in those times.

Today we're going to be looking at a rather unorthodox gold stock, Royal Gold (NASDAQ:RGLD). They're not a gold miner, like a lot of the sector, but they are gold mining adjacent. They buy precious metal streams and royalties from other peoples' mines, which simplifies things somewhat and also has been a very successful model for them so far.

We're going to be digging into this today, the business, the strong 2024 results, and what we might expect in the future, given the existing environment and the excellent price trajectory gold has been on recently. Fair warning, this will be a long one, because there's a lot to talk about here. If I do follow-ups in the future, as is my intention right now, they'll be more straightforward and not so introduction-heavy, but this is an unusual company and it's worth exploring.

Royalties vs Streams

So before we dive into things, here's a quick little introduction to royalties and how they work compared to streams.

First, let's talk royalties. The royalty owner gets a percentage of the revenue generated by the property. Given the number of royalty trusts out there in the oil market, I assume everyone has a rough understanding of how that works, so I won't delve too deep into it.

Streams, of the other hand, are a lot more unique, and seem really appealing in a bull market for gold. Owners of a stream have the first right to buy a portion of the precious metal the mine produces, often at a substantial discount to market price. Some of these are a percentage of the present spot price of the metal, while others are a flat dollar amount. Clearly, if the spot price increases substantially, having a locked in flat rate could become incredibly valuable.

Where Royal Gold Comes Into This

So royalties and streams both sound interesting, but let's talk Royal Gold. The company acquires some existing streams and royalty rights from others, but also helps finance some projects in exchange for the streams or royalties. As said before, they don't do any mining themselves, and as of 2024, their portfolio's revenue generation was 41.2% streams, and 58.8% royalties. The properties are located all over the world.

Now going through all the properties Royal Gold has interests in would take forever, and this is already going to be a long enough introduction as it is, so we'll just take a look at the four largest interests, as the company emphasizes their import in their conference calls, and they seem a good sample of the overall business. These properties represented 55% of Royal Gold's revenue in 2024.

Those Principal Properties

Carmen de Andacollo, which is operated by Teck, is an open-pit copper-gold mine with a sulfide flotation mill. It is located in central Chile. It's mostly a copper producer, though Royal Gold, perhaps unsurprising given their name, has no interest in the copper itself.

Royal Gold has a streaming right on 100% of the gold produced at Carmen de Andacollo. They are able to buy every single ounce produced at 15% of the monthly average price for gold. With gold prices around $3,000 an ounce, that translates to a cost for Royal Gold of $450 an ounce. Prices are a bit higher than that lately, so the cost is proportionately higher too, but the discount remains, and is substantial. That's basically why streams are so nice in this case, Royal Gold is effectively guaranteed to make 85% of the average monthly price, whatever that turns out to be at any given time.

Next up we have Mount Milligan, which, much like Carmen de Andacollo, is an open-pit copper-gold mine with a sulfide flotation mill to process. Unlike the Andacollo site, Mount Milligan is in north central British Columbia, Canada. The mine is run by Centerra Gold.

Once again, Royal Gold has streaming rights, but also unlike Andacollo, they have rights on both the gold and the copper. On the gold front, Royal Gold has a right to purchase 35% of the gold produced at Mount Milligan, at a price of $435 an ounce. No percentage here, so while it translates to just under 14% of the current spot price. If the price of gold continues to rise, the cost here remains the same.

With regard to the copper, Royal Gold has a right to buy 18.75% of the copper Mount Milligan produces at 15% of the spot price. This is quite valuable too, because Mount Milligan produces lot of copper, and with such a discount on it, you can't help but turn a profit.

On a somewhat different, but no less important front, we have Pueblo Viejo, an open pit gold and silver mine in the central part of the Dominican Republic. They are run by a joint venture of Barrick and Newmont Mining. The site has an oxidation and cyanide leaching plant.

The terms are a lot different here, though still streams. Royal Gold has a right to purchase 7.5% of the gold at the start, and after they get 990,000 ounces, they get to purchase 3.75% afterwards. The prices are somewhat less appealing than the first two. They can buy the gold at 30% of spot price until they reach 550,000 ounces, and then 60% of the spot price afterward.

And of course, Royal Gold also has silver streaming rights in Pueblo Viejo as well. That's very important, as we'll soon see. On the surface, they have the right to buy 75% of the first 50 million ounces of silver, and 37.5% of the rest. The price is 30% of spot on the first 23.1 million ounces, and 60% afterward.

Why that's particularly important is that this silver stream guarantees them a certain amount of silver, and if the mine isn't reaching that production, they get deferred rights, where they can effectively buy all the silver until the mine catches up. That's key here because in the 10-K, Royal Gold reported 1.7 million ounces have been deferred. If it gets delivered a lot at once, because maybe the mine hits an unusual silver rich spot within it's mine, that could lead to a major revenue increase.

Finally, we have the Cortez complex in Nevada. Strap in for this one because they've got a whole slew of different sites within the complex, and once again it's a gold and silver mining site, some open pit and some underground, owned by another joint Barrick-Newmont Mining venture. This includes a cyanide leaching mill, but also other heap leaching facilities.

As you can see, it amounts to a lot of different sites, and different zones come with different rates. Fortunately, Royal Gold's interest here isn't streams because that would take all day to break down. In this case, it's a collection of royalty rights. In some properties, they have a right to as much as 9.4% of the revenue of the operations, and in other sites that drops to as low as 0.45%.

According to the conference call, Barrick said they expect a substantial increase in gold produced at Cortez in the next few years, from around 680,000 to 765,000 ounces in 2025 all the way up to over one million ounces in 2027. As I said, Royal Gold has a lot more than just those four sites, but those are their most important ones. Other smaller and even prospective sites are here too. Overall, Royal Gold's revenue in 2024 was 76% gold-related, 12% silver, 9% copper and 3% from other minerals.

2024: Another Success Story

You can see from those streaming discounts they get and the royalties they receive that it would be very hard not to make a profit doing what Royal Gold does. Indeed, it's not just profitable, but wildly so, and the margins can be enormous.

2022

2023

2024

Revenue
